one_line_takeaway: A protocol-level 'trust layer' that makes every agentic tool/data boundary crossing a cryptographically signed, policy-bound invocation verified by embedded PEPs -- yielding deterministic (by construction) enforcement with 100% recall / 0 false positives on the authors' own 174-case suite and 9-framework coverage, though the guarantees rest on strong trust assumptions (immutable frameworks, correct admin-authored policies) and self-constructed rather than comparative evaluation.
key_finding: On the authors' own 174-case attack suite, cryptographic-plus-policy enforcement achieves 100% recall with 0% false positives, and covers 9 of 10 OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 risks (LLM04 data-poisoning only partially; LLM09 misinformation deemed tangential to security). The two production CVEs (OpenAI Atlas browser prompt-injection credential exfiltration; GitHub MCP server CVE-2025-6514, 558,846+ downloads) are, in emulation, completely blocked -- attacks that reportedly bypass semantic guardrails are stopped deterministically because a filesystem policy denying *credential* paths rejects the operation regardless of a valid signature from the (compromised) LLM. Cryptographic overhead is sub-millisecond and negligible against network latency. The central conceptual claim: because enforcement reduces to breaking cryptographic primitives rather than pattern-matching, detection is deterministic ('operations either carry valid cryptographic proof or are rejected') rather than probabilistic.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'authenticated workflows,' a protocol-level trust layer for enterprise agentic AI that reframes agentic security as the problem of protecting four fundamental boundary crossings: prompts (S1), tools (S2), data (S3), and context (S4). At every boundary, an operation must satisfy two properties: intent (it satisfies organizational policy) and integrity (it is cryptographically authentic). Each inter-entity invocation is turned into Invocation = (Args, Policy, MAC) where MAC = Sign(K, Args || Policy), verified by distributed Policy Enforcement Points (PEPs) embedded at each control surface. Two named sub-contributions are the paper's own: (1) MAPL, an 'AI-native' policy language whose policies compose via intersection semantics (allow = R1 intersect R2, deny = D1 union D2, most-restrictive constraints) with cryptographic attestations expressing workflow dependencies (e.g. 'export only after anonymization-completed attestation exists'), claimed to reduce policy specification from O(M x N) to O(log M + N) via hierarchical composition and runtime principal resolution; and (2) a universal security runtime integrating nine agent frameworks via thin adapters (200-500 LOC) requiring zero protocol changes. The paper backs this with formal proofs (Theorems 1-3 on policy composition, Lemmas 1-7 on integrity/intent/completeness) and empirical attack testing. NOTE: prompt/context protection itself is largely delegated to the authors' companion paper [3] ('Protecting context and prompts'); this paper's own focus is control-flow protection at tool invocations and data retrievals (S2/S3).
threat_model: Explicitly formalized (Section 2). Adversary capabilities: A1 application-layer code control; A2 content injection into data/prompts/context; A3 component compromise incl. obtaining private keys; A4 network intercept/replay/modify; A5 compositional/gradual attacks spanning interactions or framework boundaries. Trust assumptions: L1 cryptographic hardness (unforgeable signatures); L2 a trusted minimal control plane (Agent Registry, Policy Store, Logging, Routing with hash-chained logs and Merkle-tree policy store); L3 enforcement integrity (PEP logic executes correctly at immutable framework boundaries). Explicitly OUT OF SCOPE: bypassing PEPs by compromising framework binaries, kernel-level attacks, debuggers, and memory corruption (treated as 'equivalent to kernel compromise'), and application-level content safety (e.g. detecting malicious code). Security objectives O1-O5: integrity, policy enforcement, privilege non-escalation, context integrity, accountability. The paper claims O1-O5 hold against A1-A5 under L1-L3.
limitations: (1) The headline '100% recall / 0 false positives' is measured on the authors' OWN constructed test suite built around their own attack taxonomy; there is no head-to-head comparison against baseline defenses on the SAME test set. The contrasting '60-80% detection' figure for semantic guardrails is CITED from prior work (Related Work), not a measured baseline in this paper -- do not attribute it as a comparative experimental result. (2) The '100% recall' is partly definitional: since any operation lacking a valid signature or violating policy is rejected by construction, recall is high by design on tests framed around this model; effectiveness depends entirely on administrators writing correct policies ('we provide the mechanism -- end users supply the policy'). (3) CVE 'mitigations' are emulated/reconstructed scenarios, not tests against the live production systems. (4) Strong trust assumptions: L3 requires immutable frameworks and uncorruptable PEP memory; framework-binary/kernel/memory-corruption attacks are declared out of scope -- a real deployment where frameworks are not immutable may not satisfy this. (5) Formal results are proof SKETCHES; completeness/minimality of the four surfaces (Lemma 6) is argued by informal enumeration. (6) The deterministic guarantee covers only pipeline Stages 1-3; the optional LLM-based custom verifiers are probabilistic and add 150-500 ms, and could themselves introduce false positives. (7) Performance is microbenchmark-only; full system/deployment evaluation and per-framework integration cost are deferred to an unspecified companion paper. (8) No artifact/code availability or reproducibility statement. (9) Prompt/context protection (S1/S4) leans heavily on companion paper [3], so this paper alone does not fully establish end-to-end four-surface protection.

datasets_used: Self-constructed attack test suite: 174 test cases across 11 attack patterns, 18 explicit variants, 8 mechanism categories (6 explicitly implemented), spanning framework combinations (OpenAI, Anthropic/Claude, LangChain, etc.) and configuration variants
OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 risk taxonomy (mapping/coverage analysis, Table 2)
Two production CVE case studies (emulated): OpenAI Atlas agentic browser prompt-injection (Oct 2025); GitHub MCP server CVE-2025-6514 (558,846+ downloads)
Performance microbenchmarks on commodity hardware: 8-core CPU, 16GB RAM, ECDSA-256 signatures, SHA-256 hashing
quantitative_results: 100% recall with 0% (zero) false positives across 174 test cases
9 of 10 OWASP Top 10 for LLM Apps 2025 risks covered (LLM04 partial: runtime poisoning prevented, training-data out of scope; LLM09 tangential)
9 frameworks integrated via thin adapters of 200-500 lines of code, requiring zero protocol modifications
MAPL policy specification scaling reduced from O(M x N) to O(log M + N), with organizational branching factor 8-16
ECDSA-256: signature generation ~102 microseconds, verification ~89 microseconds; hash-chain updates <15 microseconds per operation; total crypto overhead ~0.2 ms
Policy retrieval latency: in-memory ~5-50 microseconds, filesystem ~30-200 microseconds, Redis ~50-300 microseconds; policy intersection <100 microseconds for typical 3-5 deep hierarchy
Custom verifiers: deterministic checks add 30-400 microseconds; LLM-based prompt-safety verifiers add 150-500 ms (dominant when enabled)
Total local PEP verification overhead <1 ms without LLM verifiers; ~0.2 ms crypto overhead negligible vs 50-500 ms network latency
Complete mitigation (in emulation) of 2 production CVEs that bypassed baseline semantic guardrails
GitHub MCP server compromised via CVE-2025-6514 had 558,846+ downloads
baselines_compared: Semantic guardrails / prompt filters (NeMo Guardrails, Rebuff, Llama Guard) -- cited as achieving 60-80% detection but remaining bypassable; NOT run head-to-head in this paper (comparison is qualitative/from literature)
Policy engines OPA and AWS Cedar -- discussed as composing via intersection but lacking attestation-based temporal workflow dependencies, dynamic principals, and cross-surface cryptographic binding
Identity systems SPIFFE and AWS IAM -- strong identity but no workflow semantics
Platform/measurement attestation TPM and Intel SGX -- prove code integrity, contrasted with MAPL attestations that prove workflow completion
Framework-native security (LangChain schemas, MCP auth/permissions, OpenAI rate limits/moderation) -- cited as failing at cross-framework composition
Prior/companion work: authors' own [3] handling prompts+context; authenticated system calls [5] and inline reference monitors [6] extended from kernel to four control surfaces
